narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30 and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put together a thoughtful application, including your middle-class family background and school decile rankings from earlier years. However, the admissions model predicted a 54% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which tipped the decision against admitting you right now. This prediction relies on patterns from thousands of past students, focusing on key indicators of bar success like test scores and grades. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score of 25, well below the typical 36.7 average, as lower scores like yours have historically correlated strongly with bar struggles in the model's analysis. Countering that somewhat was your undergraduate GPA, which at 3.30 exceeds the average of 3.21 and pulled the prediction toward expecting you to pass. Your Year 3 school decile ranking of 4 also helped a bit in the positive direction, though it's slightly below the average 5.53, showing some academic resilience compared to peers. Overall, the LSAT's heavy negative weight overshadowed these strengths, making the model see higher risk in your profile than in typical admitted students who pass the bar. While full-time status is common and didn't sway things much, your middle-income background aligned with averages and stayed neutral. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a standard background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above average, as that single change could flip predictions for schools using this model. Strengthening any weak grade deciles through additional coursework or explanations in reapplications might also help. With persistence, you can reapply stronger next cycle and turn this into just a stepping stone.
